data.title: Diebold Nixdorf: On Course To Become The Next Great Compounder
data.description:
Diebold Nixdorf (DBD) has quietly been putting up strong returns over the years and has a simple offering—they are one of the largest manufacturers of ATMs globally, serving banks and credit unions. YTD the stock is already up another 22%, and for a SMID-cap name in this market, that's an incredible level of performance. The natural question is to wonder whether this run can continue, especially now that the stock has a more premium valuation. Simply put, Diebold appears to be capable of continuing to produce solid returns as they transform from a legacy ATM manufacturer into a digital and services-led business after exiting bankruptcy three years ago.

Company Overview

Counterintuitive as it is, this is a company that understands that shifting landscape and has taken steps to still go after opportunities. They are the #1 manufacturer globally for ATMs installed and ATM application and monitoring software, and they work with the top 100 financial institutions in the world and the top 25 retailers in Europe.

To understand this business, let's take a step back. They have two segments - banking and retail—at a 74%/26% split. On a solution basis, their revenue is driven 57% by services and 43% by products, and gross profit has a similar mix from solutions, just a slightly higher uptick coming from services. Importantly, 70% of that services revenue is recurring. The company has an installed base of nearly 800,000 ATMs worldwide across 100 countries, which requires maintenance frequently. I will note that they have a large European presence, too.

You can think of banking as all of your traditional ATM operations - walking into your local branch and seeing the ATM and its corresponding software; there's a good chance it's a DBD machine. On the retail side, Diebold provides self-checkout (SCO) machines, kiosks (like the stand-up ones at fast food restaurants), and the software behind all of it.

Business Analysis

What was interesting to me last year is the slide they provided with the TAM. They noted that there's a combined $32 billion opportunity for them, split $20 billion in banking and $12 billion in retail. The opportunity for the retail side of the business is likely being understated, as the rollout of automated checkout at retail locations globally has been slower than expected the last few years as theft has increased at the point of sale. That being said, you have a low-single-digit growth framework for banking and a mid-single-digit growth framework for retail.

Source: Investor Presentation

This is a company that did ~$3.8 billion in sales in totality in FY25, which would imply that they have a very small market share relative to the TAM they provided (just a high-single-digit market share). This would imply a heavily fragmented market, but that seems rather odd, as there don't seem to be too many companies entering into the ATM-manufacturing business in 2026! See, the issue is that in banking, there are locked contracts for legacy providers and a long tail of independent ATM deployers, effectively creating minimal room until contracts expire for DBD to win business. At the same time, on the retail front, there is intense competition from players like NCR Voyix and Toshiba.

To be clear, this company is not telling you that the installed base of global ATMs is growing - they had put out that a 5-year trajectory would be "stable" effectively a 0% change vs. the 2024 baseline at about 2 million units. It wasn't stated explicitly in their investor deck last year, but after seeing the EM market figures (particularly for India and the Middle East) for ATM unit growth, it likely implies that N. America is down, which makes sense. I had previously said they have 800k installed units, which implies that DBD's market share is actually about 40%. The reason the TAM is so large is because of the services revenue. The only real opportunity they have on the hardware side at this point is upgrading existing systems as contracts expire, and they believe that opportunity is about 600,000 units, but they have not placed a timeline around it. There is little to ascribe from this type of an opportunity in the near term for DBD, in my view.

Thus, banking services and global SCO seem to be the more interesting opportunities. Beginning with the latter, SCO is growing its installed base globally over the next five years at a 10% CAGR. Self-checkout is increasingly prevalent in grocery stores, fast food, and mainline retail, and there is a clear, positive response from the consumer to not have to interact socially when purchasing goods. What's interesting about SCO is that it's actually been more of a European growth story than a US - the opportunity now shifts domestically. They are the #1 market share player in EMEA but have a very small market share here in the US. There is the potential for aggressive expansion on the US side, and it may be complemented by M&A in order to achieve scale quicker by 2029. Banking services, on the other hand, get to grow at a price/cost mix formula, essentially - for whatever labor costs go up by and software costs go up by, the company can charge its banking clients. This creates a staple-ish demand profile, and because it doesn't make sense to go external with the maintenance on a machine for large institutions (they don't want to be contacting dozens of non-experts on highly complex machines, let alone ones that involve currency), the company has achieved a 90% service attach rate.

Thus, the takeaway should be that there are two very tangible opportunities for this company for growth - banking services and SCO, particularly in North America over the projection period through 2029. In an industry with higher barriers to entry.

Financial & Balance Sheet Assessment

Up to this point, the opportunities sound decent for DBD in both the short term and through the end of the decade, even within an industry that faces structural challenges for long-term unit growth in North American ATMs. Opportunities can sound great, but the bigger question is, what takes DBD to a $5 billion market cap and provides real value for shareholders? Can this story scale? Last year, the company held an Investor Day for which it set out double-digit adjusted EBITDA growth by 2027 and 60% FCF conversion.

Let's unpack guidance - the company just offered the following raised guidance:

  • Total Revenue: $3.86 billion–$3.94 billion
    • for which, banking & retail segments +low-single-digits YoY
    • Back half of year revenue weighting 45% 1H/55% 2H
  • Adjusted EBITDA: $510 million-$535 million
  • Free Cash Flow: $255 million-$270 million
  • Adjusted EPS: $5.25-$5.75

It should be noted that management raised their revenue targets modestly (about a point at the midpoint) while also coming up $10 million on the adjusted EBITDA low-end/$5 million on the high-end.

The free cash flow profile is interesting. At the midpoint, the guidance would imply that this is trading just south of a 10% FCF yield. Despite this being at the lower end of the range in just the last year, this is the first time we can really start to validate the free cash flow since they exited the bankruptcy process 3 years ago.

Source: Compiled by Author

Opex seems rather disciplined for the year ahead. They have a savings plan with over 200 actions that results in opex down 1-2% YoY. This is half of their plan's benefit, which means opex into FY27 should also be modeled down another 1-2%. Now, let's say that they're able to achieve the midpoint of their adjusted EBITDA guide, which would imply about $522 million. Their Investor Day target from last year purports another 10% growth, at least, on top of that $522 million, which would imply ~$575 million in FY27 adjusted EBITDA. Street estimates are sitting at $520 million/$575 million for FY26/FY27, respectively. Now, the company last year had actually already provided 3-year targets, and we're right there at the $575 million midpoint, seen in the below slide deck:

Source: Investor Presentation

Capital allocation seems to be aligned with shareholder value. They have a $200 million share repurchase authorization in place. That's a high-single-digit buyback yield, rather large for a company like this.

That prompts a discussion of the balance sheet. Rarely do we get to talk about balance sheet activity being catalysts, but this is certainly in that camp. The company's credit rating is B+/B1, which is high yield, i.e., below investment grade. This means their financing and borrowing costs are at a disadvantage relative to investment-grade companies. Credit ratings tend to be based upon FCF conversion rates, net leverage, and business outlook. Last year they achieved 49% FCF conversion and are targeting north of 50% this year. That's not enough of a delta to move the needle on the credit rating, but what is their leverage profile.

At 1.1x net leverage, the company has a key maturity of $950 million in senior notes due later in the year during Q4. This is important because they'll need to refi these. Now, if the company achieves its FCF guidance of nearly $262 million, then cash assets are going to improve to ~$678 million. With a successful refinancing of their '26 senior notes, you could be looking at sub-1x leverage for the first time in years on an LTM basis.

Two things here: there's scope for a credit rating upgrade, which, while a debt-side event, could positively influence the stock. Getting closer to investment grade means opening up to a larger pool of investors, and I suspect this could happen in Q1 of fiscal 2027 for DBD. The credit rating agencies are likely waiting to see how the refinancing goes before moving them up a notch. I see this as a key catalyst for shares. Now, recognize that business outlook plays an important role in credit rating agencies' decisions, but this is not the Diebold of old - this is clearly a company that has pivoted out of its legacy hardware base and towards a highly recurring service model with clear growth opportunities both domestic and international. It's difficult to see this not happening, quite frankly.

Valuation & Risks

We cannot talk about valuation without comparing DBD to NCR Voyix (VYX), which has plummeted in the last 6 months, down a whopping 48.5%, creating a rather large alpha spread for DBD vs. VYX. VYX is moderately smaller than DBD, but the fundamentals are two entirely different stories. VYX coming into their current fiscal year was already 2.1x net levered, and on an ex-hardware transaction basis, their growth guide of +1% at the midpoint (range: -2% to +3%) and the company is going through a messy restructuring. Adjusted EBITDA and earnings growth are still there for NCR, but the appetite from the market for an unproven software player warrants a lower multiple. If Voyix is going to be a software competitor, it needs to be growing at double digits and have FCF conversion in the high 80s to 90% range, not retain the conversion rates that an asset-intensive business has. There is little reason to get excited about that one right now until the turnaround actually looks to have legs.

Source: Compiled by Author

In the last year for Diebold, the company has seen a 120% FCF gain and 60bps of adjusted EBITDA margin expansion on just a 1.5% revenue growth figure. Now, the company has guided revenue conservatively after a lumpy year last year and a weak Q1, which serves as an easy comparison, especially going into the current quarter with the momentum from favorable geographic and product mix. With a mid-single-digit growth algorithm, there could be a guidance raise in the cards as we get into mid-year. If DBD starts to do mid-single-digit revenue growth, incremental adjusted EBITDA margins would start to support adjusted EBITDA revenue estimates in the $540 million range this year and $600 million next year, especially with more services-based revenue on the come and North American SCO scaling.

At $600 million, that's mid-single-digit upside vs. street estimates, and you're looking at a valuation for the stock of 5.4x on a net basis on FY27 numbers. That's rather cheap for a mid-single-digit growth algorithm and a now proven ability for management to bring in highly recurring, high-margin services-based revenue. This seems to be mispriced, even post the run. There has to be a bit of understanding given the history - the company filed for bankruptcy in August of 2023 and exited in October of 2023 as rising interest rates got the better of them. Insolvency concerns are well behind the company at this point.

Source: Compiled by Author

Conclusion

Diebold seems set up to produce modest upside to street estimates in the coming quarters, but more importantly, the company could see a ratings upgrade from credit rating agencies in the coming quarter as free cash flow conversion improves, the business model transitions more to services revenue, and leverage falls below 1x on a net basis. This should reduce the overall risk profile of the stock and enable financial stability for years to come. With a mid-single-digit percentage growth algorithm on the horizon, there is little reason to not be involved, even after the run DBD has had since its 2023 bankruptcy.

data.title: SES AI Has Positive 2026 Outlook, But Risk Has Increased (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
SES AI Corporation (SES) reported weaker financial performance for Q4 last month. Lower revenue and a change in revenue mix spooked investors, and the stock dropped nearly $0.70 over two days. The price ultimately dipped below $1.00 per share and has not fully recovered.

SES AI will report Q1 results later this month, and the company gave an update on its expectations. The outlook is positive, but it may not be enough to change the investor outlook. There is more risk going forward because revenue will change, and new sources of revenue have not been fully established. The company has only reported revenue for five quarters, so the situation is delicate.

The stock price is up 79% over the last year. The price is down 48% since I covered the company last December on Seeking Alpha. Q1 results are expected to be strong, and the price may begin a new uptrend. For now, I change my rating from a Strong Buy to a Hold. Strong revenue momentum will need to be reestablished for the rating to change.

Q1-2026 Expectations

SES AI recently shared its guidance for Q1-2026 revenue. The company expects between $6.3 and $6.5 million in revenue. Revenue from UZ Energy is expected to drive the reports. Revenue of $1.5 million from delayed Q4 shipments will also be included. The amount is above market consensus, which predicts $3.65 million in revenue. The company will report on April 23 post-market. SES AI expects the 2026 fiscal year revenue to be between $30 million and $35 million.

Current Synergies

Revenue dropped for Q4-2025 because the company completed its current contracts with Honda and Hyundai to develop B sample EV batteries. There will be no further demand for samples until the companies move to mass production of the batteries, i.e., the C sample stage. SES AI does not expect it to happen anytime soon. Revenue from B samples will not be present for 2026. This development likely spooked investors.

The news is not as bad as it seems. The company has other sources of revenue and will scale some of its operations. SES AI will now collect revenue from three segments: ESS (Energy Storage Systems) sales, drone battery sales, and advanced material sales. The company says that its biggest current synergy for 2026 will be revenue from its ESS hardware segment via UZ Energy, which will make up 65% of revenue going forward.

The company feels that the market for drone batteries will increase greatly due to the military use of drones. SES AI will convert its South Korea factory from EV cell manufacture to NDAA-compliant drone cell manufacture. The company is considering the production of NDAA-compliant drone batteries at an additional facility in Asia. Since this conversion is in the process, it is unclear how much revenue the company will bring in from drone battery sales. SES AI already has potential clients lined up.

SES AI will also manufacture and sell advanced materials for electric batteries to OEMs. The company has a joint venture with Hisun to produce and distribute its battery materials on a commercial scale. SES AI, via its Molecular Universe AI platform, has discovered and made six new battery prototypes, which are currently being tested.

The company’s Molecular Universe AI platform is still being heavily used by the company and is being licensed out to other companies. It will bring in some revenue, but the platform will not be the major contributor just yet. SES AI foresees the expansion of its AI platform capabilities into other molecular discoveries for different applications and sectors.

ESS sales will become the new major contributor to revenue. The company will produce its energy systems equipped with monitoring software. The software, driven by AI, will give metrics about the battery’s degradation, health, and stage. SES AI plans to enter the US markets with its ESS products. The company acquired UZ Energy two quarters ago. UZ Energy manufactures and sells ESS on a global basis, including Southeast Asia, the Middle East, and Europe.

The company has good synergies and a positive 2016 outlook, but some of these revenues are still being established and lack performance history in terms of revenue capability. It is likely that these changes with the company and the lower revenue for Q4 caused some investors to take profits and move on. The company has only been reporting revenue for the last five quarters, which is not enough to establish much of a trend. The long-term outlook for the company is very strong.

Q4-2025 Results

SES AI reported $4.6 million in Q4-2025 revenue, an increase of 124% YoY and a decrease of 35% QoQ. Gross profit was $0.5 million, a decrease of 60% YoY and a decrease of 86% QoQ. Profit margins were 11.27% versus 63.14% YoY and 51.08% QoQ. Lower margins were due to product revenue being dominant instead of service revenue. Lower revenue was due to the completion of the Honda-Hyundai contract.

Net loss was $17.0 million versus a net loss of 34.5 million QoQ and a net loss of $20.9 million QoQ. Cash and ST investments at the end of Q4 were $199.6 million.

The company used $10.4 million in cash for operations during Q4. The amount includes $3.3 million for the UZ Energy acquisition, $2.9 million on CapEx, and $1.6 million in stock buybacks. The company is debt-free.

Revenue and gross profit decreased QoQ. Operating expenses have decreased. Net loss is persistent and will remain until revenue has increased beyond operating costs. The balance sheet is strong. Assets outweigh liabilities.

The company is overvalued in terms of its current metrics. The stock price is higher than its book value per share and the NAV per share. Market cap is higher than total EV. Valuation should improve with higher revenues.

Investment Strategy and Risk

SES AI is at low to moderate risk of poor financial performance. There are a lot of unknowns in the short term, but the company has plenty of assets and liquidity to continue to scale up operations. The company’s assets far outweigh its liabilities, and there is no debt. So far, the company has shown strong performance, and there is no reason to think that it will change.

A Hold rating for SES AI comes at a low to moderate risk. The price has already dropped since last month, but it has shown some stability and recovery since then. The Q1 outlook is strong, and the stock may rally on the news. Greater stock market volatility has begun to subside, and AI-related stocks are uptrending again.

Conclusion

SES AI saw its stock drop last month over its Q4-2025 earnings. Earlier revenue performance came from contracts that have now ended. The company is relying on revenue from its ESS segment for Q1. The company updated its Q1 expectations, and the number will beat market expectations. There is some uncertainty in that the company is still fully establishing its other two revenue streams, which consist of drone battery sales and advanced battery materials sales. I rate the company as a Hold and consider that the company has long-term potential.

data.title: CF Industries: Shares Are Higher For Longer
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Shares of fertilizer giant CF Industries (CF) have risen 80.3%, including dividends, since the last time I wrote on the company almost 2 years ago.

CF Industries is in the right place at the right time. The company’s nitrogen-based fertilizer is the foundation of American farming, and thanks to the ongoing war in Ukraine plus the war with Iran, the gap in input prices between what CF has to pay to make their fertilizer in the US and what their competitors have to pay abroad is durable.

In essence, CF has a geological moat that is giving them huge cost advantages. I don’t expect this to go away anytime soon, even if the Strait of Hormuz opens up or the war in Ukraine ends quickly.

With this, I think shares make sense at this valuation. In fact, I actually think they have more upside from here. I believe shares remain a strong buy despite their strong market outperformance and the 56% YTD gain.

Why I’m Doing Follow-Up Coverage

While the market has now fully recovered from the start of the war with Iran, CF Industries has been a perfect example of what some would call an ‘anti-fragile’ stock. The company has generated a total return (including dividends) of 80% since the last time I wrote about them in the summer of 2024.

Some analysts on Seeking Alpha (like Bashar Issa) rate the company a hold on the concerns that if China were to allow fertilizer exports again, or if Russia and Ukraine were to end their war, the company’s competitive cost edge would disappear. Personally, I disagree.

I believe we are in a new paradigm for global nitrogen fertilizer production, and I still don’t think the market has priced this in. It leaves CF Industries’ management with a unique opportunity to continue to buy back shares at what I think is a steep discount to their fair value.

I’m doing follow-up coverage because I believe CF Industries has the potential to be a long-run compounder that is just getting started.

Higher For Longer

To understand why I think shares have room to be ‘higher for longer’ in a market that is (in theory) commoditized, I want to show what makes the company’s current production so lucrative.

If we look at the company’s Q3 2025 earnings, management set aside a key chart that shows their positive economic spread they have by using Henry Hub natural gas (US natural gas) to make their fertilizer, while their international competitors have to use higher market averages.

Keep in mind that this spread has gotten wider since the start of the war with Iran, given that so much of the world’s natural gas is produced in the Middle East and its shipments have been disrupted.

Notably, while many analysts on Wall Street have been expecting more supply to come online in the US to increase the capacity of low-cost nitrogen-based fertilizers, this hasn’t happened.

…you have a supply limited market and the expectation that this new capacity in the United States, Gulf Coast and Woodside will be coming on. But we're 3 years now waiting for some of that capacity to come on and demand continues to grow. And we continue to supply some of that with our new low carbon product as well as just participating in the market today is $600 to $700 a ton for ammonia on the globally traded market. And our cost today at $3 gas is about $120, $130. And so it's very attractive for us today.

-Berf Frost, CCO of CF Industries at Bank of America Conference

With this core margin, CF Industries has been able to push capital into share buybacks, which I believe are a key part of their secret sauce to make a commodity stock a compounder.

In fact, by Q4, management noted that the company has been able to reduce overall share count by 57% since 2010 while at the same time increasing nitrogen production by 36%.

The company is doing more than taking legacy infrastructure and spending the profits on share buybacks. They’re also investing in production growth. I believe this is paying off.

Putting it all together, and once again, as CCO Frost noted:

[analysts have been] negative, negative, negative, but we've had a positive, positive, positive market where we've exceeded expectations each year since 2021 in terms of an EBITDA performance or a free cash flow performance.

While fertilizer is niche, I think this dichotomy between sell-side analysts keeping ratings down on shares while the core fertilizer market remains so strong has created one of the best risk-reward setups out there (more on this in the valuation section).

When Wall Street expects the shoe to drop, but instead the stock is incredibly strong, it opens up the opportunity for a rapid rerating of the shares both by analysis and, more importantly, by the market. I think we’re near this point.

Valuation

While CF has seen its stock trounce the market this year, I continue to believe there is more upside for the fertilizer giant.

On a forward valuation multiple, CF trades at 9.25 times forward GAAP earnings. Not only is this 12.92% lower than the 5-year median for the equity, but it's also 45.59% lower than the sector median for the materials sector.

In essence, Wall Street continues to value CF like it's a commoditized fertilizer player. Not true. CF has now been benefitting from 4+ years of a favorable natural gas spread. Because the US continues to be in a shale boom, and because global sources of LNG look like they face geopolitical roadblocks (such as Qatar), I expect this favorable spread to continue.

With this, I think shares need to trade closer to the sector median forward GAAP P/E. The earnings are durable; the multiple should reflect that.

If we saw shares trade at a forward P/E of 17 (the sector median), this would mean 83.78% upside from here in shares.

Risks

I think CF Industries is in a solid spot. Their favorable natural gas spreads are one of the strongest tailwinds any nitrogen-based fertilizer company could ask for. At the same time, this is the single biggest risk: what if global prices normalize after years of war in Ukraine and now the war with Iran?

To look at this deeper, we need to see what CF Industries’ earnings were before the war.

Pre-COVID and pre-war in Ukraine, earnings were volatile as a result of a highly efficient global natural gas market that had the company swinging from loss to breaking even to marginally profitable from late 2015 all the way to 2021.

In essence, since the war in Ukraine started, the market has been betting that global chaos will melt away, the world will go back to normal, and this type of earnings performance will return.

I disagree. Because, unfortunately, the world is not returning to ‘normal.’ This is the new normal.

For investors, look at countries like Qatar. They produce (by some estimates) 20% of the world's LNG. Their major facilities have been hit by the attacks by Iran. Qatar's energy company (QatarEnergy) believes this could take up to 5 years to rebuild.

In essence, while a peace deal could theoretically happen at any time, there is no guarantee that CF Industries would see their favorable natural gas spread compress anytime soon.

This is why I think their earnings are higher for longer. The market has structurally changed. I think the biggest bear thesis assumes that things return to a pre-pandemic normal.

Bottom Line

While CF Industries has been one of the top performers in the S&P 500 this year, I don’t think the rally is done. I believe what we are seeing now (as we did 4 years ago when the war in Ukraine broke out) is a fundamental shift in how the world prices natural gas risk. Modern supply chains are used to ‘just in time’ manufacturing and ‘just in time’ supplies. Two major wars in 4 years flip this model on its head. Companies need to think more about how they source energy. This only makes the spread more favorable for CF Industries.

The company benefits from being primarily a North American producer of nitrogen-based fertilizers, in a world where global fertilizer producers are going out of business due to unfavorable natural gas spreads.

While geography is rarely a ‘moat’ in a global commoditized space like fertilizer, I ironically think it is and will continue to be for CF Industries.

With this, I continue to believe that shares are a strong buy.

data.title: Strait Of Hormuz Disruption And Global Supply Implications
data.description:
From record gold prices to a fertilizer supply shock, the Strait of Hormuz closure reshaped commodity markets in Q1 2026, accelerating a structural shift in how global resources are valued.

A Geopolitical Shock Exposes the Cost of Supply Fragility

Q1 2026 was defined by the outbreak of hostilities between the United States and Iran on February 28, which effectively closed the Strait of Hormuz, a chokepoint for roughly one-fifth of global oil and LNG trade. What began as a quarter of strong metals momentum and disciplined portfolio rotation ended with historic oil price volatility, a cascading fertilizer supply shock and a sharp precious metals correction that partially reversed January’s record-setting gains. The effects across commodities, equities and supply chains will likely prove structural rather than transitory.

Sector Performance Recap

Oil & Gas

Oil entered the quarter near $61/bbl Brent on a well-supplied market before the Strait of Hormuz closure sent prices surging past $117/bbl. Iranian drone strikes on Qatar’s Ras Laffan LNG hub simultaneously disrupted roughly 20% of global LNG supply. By late March, diplomatic progress drove a sharp reversal toward $67 as risk premiums compressed. Energy was the only major equity sector to finish the quarter in positive territory, with refiners, integrated majors, low-cost E&Ps and MENA-exposed oilfield services names all benefiting from the price spike. US natural gas equities also rallied on the LNG supply disruption.

Base & Industrial Metals

Copper touched a record $13,952/MT in late January on supply disruptions, tariff front-running and AI/electrification demand, before surrendering a significant portion of those gains as the Iran conflict stoked demand destruction fears. China’s rare earth export controls were the other defining development, triggering a sharp re-rating of ex-China critical minerals producers. Diversified miners and rare earth names outperformed; pure-play copper equities broadly underperformed as the metal reversed through March despite intact long-term fundamentals.

Investing involves risk, including loss of principal; please see disclaimers on last page. Please call 800.826.2333 or visit vaneck.com for performance current to the most recent month end.

Gold & Precious Metals

Gold surged to an all-time high of $5,589/oz on January 28, driven by geopolitical escalation, dollar weakness, the Fed holding rates and continued PBoC buying. Silver briefly crossed $117/oz. A sharp March correction erased nearly 15% from peak on diplomatic progress and a firming dollar, leaving gold near $4,769/oz at quarter-end. Mining equities experienced a dramatic re-rating in January, with junior miners outperforming large-caps by approximately 13% as institutional capital rotated into the sector. PGM equities lagged the gold complex on softer pricing and operational pressures.

Agriculture

The Hormuz closure dramatically reshaped agricultural markets through fertilizer: roughly one-third of globally traded fertilizer transits the strait, and Iranian strikes on Qatar Energy’s production hubs removed nearly 40% of global nitrogen trade from the market. Nitrogen fertilizer producers were among the quarter’s top-performing equities globally. Protein processors and diversified agribusiness names with export access also performed well on improved soybean trade flows, while crop protection companies were more mixed amid continued farm margin pressure.

Renewables & Alternatives

Clean energy carried positive momentum from late-2025 rate cuts, but US policy headwinds, including IRA rollbacks and tightened tax credit timelines, weighed on domestic developers. The conflict added near-term risk-off pressure on growth names, partially offset by accelerating European urgency to expand domestic capacity. Grid construction equities were the standout performers; solar and wind developers and electrical component manufacturers were broadly pressured as growth multiples de-rated.

Paper & Forest Products

Forest products remained soft, with lumber rangebound and pulp under pressure from prior-year oversupply. US packaging showed early stabilization on e-commerce and industrial recovery, while European names continued to face demand and currency headwinds. North American containerboard equities modestly outperformed European counterparts, though valuation dispersion within packaging was wide based on integration execution and balance sheet strength.

Portfolio Performance: Drivers and Detractors

The Fund returned 16.2% versus 19.7% for the S&P Global Natural Resources Index. Oil & Gas was the dominant absolute contributor but detracted on a relative basis, as underweight exposure to integrated majors, which surged 40–85% on the oil price spike, more than offset strong refining performance from Valero Energy (1.51% of Fund net assets as of 3/31/26), Phillips 66 (2.24% of Fund net assets as of 3/31/26) and Marathon Petroleum (1.11% of Fund net assets as of 3/31/26). E&P and integrated holdings including ConocoPhillips (1.29% of Fund net assets as of 3/31/26), Canadian Natural Resources (1.35% of Fund net assets as of 3/31/26), TotalEnergies (3.21% of Fund net assets as of 3/31/26) and Chevron (2.59% of Fund net assets as of 3/31/26) contributed meaningfully, as did National Energy Services Reunited ("NESR") (0.43% of Fund net assets as of 3/31/26) on accelerating MENA activity.

Gold & Precious Metals was the second-largest absolute contributor but a modest relative detractor. Agnico Eagle (2.71% of Fund net assets as of 3/31/26), Franco-Nevada (1.95% of Fund net assets as of 3/31/26), Kinross Gold (2.41% of Fund net assets as of 3/31/26) and others contributed strongly through January, before disciplined trimming ahead of the March correction preserved gains. PGMs were the primary drag within the sector, with Impala Platinum (0.74% of Fund net assets as of 3/31/26) falling approximately 27% after the Fund’s rotation from Valterra (not held as of 3/31/26) and Barrick Mining (2.26% of Fund net assets as of 3/31/26) declining approximately 6%. Renewables & Alternatives and Oil & Gas were effectively tied as the largest relative detractors: the Fund carried approximately 5% in a sector with zero benchmark weight that returned approximately -2.8%, with Ivanhoe Electric (-26%) (0.75% of Fund net assets as of 3/31/26) and Nexans (-10%) (0.94% of Fund net assets as of 3/31/26) the primary culprits, partially offset by MasTec (+48%) (not held as of 3/31/26).

Agriculture was a strong absolute contributor but a notable relative detractor, as the Fund’s Nutrien (3.05% of Fund net assets as of 3/31/26) position was underweight the benchmark, and it held neither CF Industries (+69%) (not held as of 3/31/26) nor Yara (+41%) (not held as of 3/31/26). JBS N.V. (2.90% of Fund net assets as of 3/31/26), Archer-Daniels-Midland (1.82% of Fund net assets as of 3/31/26) and Bunge Global (1.31% of Fund net assets as of 3/31/26) were the leading contributors within the portfolio. Base & Industrial Metals detracted on both allocation and selection: the copper book underperformed as prices reversed and the Fund was absent from benchmark names including BHP (not held as of 3/31/26), Vale (not held as of 3/31/26) and Teck (not held as of 3/31/26) that performed well. Glencore (3.29% of Fund net assets as of 3/31/26), Lynas Rare Earths (1.23% of Fund net assets as of 3/31/26) and Alcoa (1.30% of Fund net assets as of 3/31/26) partially offset. Paper & Forest Products was the Fund’s best relative sector, driven by avoidance of European paper names that fell 6–12%.

Portfolio Activity: Q1 2026

Activity was notably elevated in Q1, with January and February particularly active.

Notable Adds

Company Sub-Sector Fund Weight (%) Rationale
International Paper (IP) Paper & Forest / Packaging 1.18 One of the world's largest packaging companies. We see an improving earnings outlook as the company integrates a recent acquisition and shifts toward higher-margin products.
Bunge Global (BG) Agriculture / Processors 1.31 A leading global grain and oilseed processor. Strong recent results and early cost savings from a major merger supported our decision to re-establish a position.
ConocoPhillips (COP) Oil & Gas / E&P 1.29 A large, well-run oil and gas producer with several new projects expected to come online beginning in late 2026, which should drive meaningful growth in cash flow.
Canadian Natural Resources (CNQ) Oil & Gas / E&P 1.35 A Canadian oil producer with an exceptionally long-life asset base and low operating costs. We initiated the position during a period of market dislocation related to Venezuela.
MP Materials (MP) Base Metals / Battery Minerals 0.82 The only significant rare earth producer in the US. We re-entered the position following a market mispricing, and see the company as a direct beneficiary of growing restrictions on Chinese rare earth exports.
Impala Platinum (IMP SJ) Precious Metals / PGMs 0.74 A South African platinum group metals producer offering similar commodity exposure to our prior holding, but at a lower valuation and with better potential for returning cash to shareholders.
Ovintiv (OVV) Oil & Gas / E&P 1.77 A North American oil and gas producer operating in two of the continent's most attractive basins, trading at what we consider an undemanding valuation relative to its asset quality.
Lynas Rare Earths (LYC AU) Base Metals / Battery Minerals 1.23 The largest rare earth producer outside of China, with an integrated mining and processing operation. Well-positioned to benefit as countries seek alternatives to Chinese rare earth supply.
Neo Performance Materials (NEO CN) Base Metals / Battery Minerals 0.83 One of the few companies outside China with the technology and capacity to manufacture the high-performance magnets used in electric vehicles and clean energy equipment.
Diamondback Energy (FANG) Oil & Gas / E&P 1.16 A highly efficient Permian Basin oil producer with a strong operational track record and a management team focused on returning value to shareholders.
Nat'l Energy Services Reunited (NESR) Oil & Gas / OFS 0.43 The only oilfield services company focused exclusively on the Middle East and North Africa region, where activity is accelerating, particularly in Saudi Arabia.
Vistra (VST) Utilities / IPPs 0.47 One of the largest independent power producers in the US, with a diversified generation portfolio well-suited to benefit from rising electricity demand driven by data centers and AI infrastructure.

Notable Exits

Company Sub-Sector Approx. Wt. at Exit (%) Rationale
Shell (SHEL) — partial Oil & Gas / Integrated ~2.00 We reduced our position as Shell's long-term production outlook weakens, with limited new projects to replace maturing fields and growth prospects trailing its peers.
Valterra Platinum (VALT LN) Precious Metals / PGMs ~0.90 Rotated proceeds into Impala Platinum, which offers comparable exposure to platinum group metals at a more attractive price and with stronger potential for shareholder returns.
Hormel Foods (HRL) Agriculture / Protein ~0.50 The company's financial recovery has taken longer than expected, and rising costs are adding new pressure to an already delayed earnings improvement.
First Solar (FSLR) Transitional Energy / Solar ~0.45 Exited the position as the risk-reward became less compelling; we may revisit if the valuation and industry backdrop become more attractive.
WaterBridge Infrastructure (WBI) Industrials / Water ~0.25 A well-run business, but with the stock near fair value and limited near-term upside, we redeployed the capital into higher-conviction opportunities.

Source: VanEck, FactSet. Data as of March 31, 2026. Not a recommendation to buy or sell any securities referenced herein. Estimated contributions are sourced from FactSet and are not intended as a predictor or guarantee of future results and are for illustrative purposes only. Portfolio compositions are subject to change at any time.

Outlook

The events of Q1 2026 have not merely introduced a new risk premium into commodity markets; they have structurally accelerated a transition already underway. What is emerging is a “resilience premium,” a durable revaluation of assets that provide secure, regionally assured access to critical resources. The Hormuz crisis removed nearly 40% of global nitrogen trade, disrupted 20% of LNG supply and introduced uncertainty across aluminum, chemicals and nickel processing. Every industry is now being forced to price in supply security that simply did not exist two years ago.

In energy, the structural case for natural gas as a bridging fuel has strengthened. The conflict demonstrated the limits of OPEC+ as a buffer, its 206,000 b/d increase was a rounding error against the scale of disruption, reinforcing the importance of domestic non-OPEC supply development. In metals, China’s rare earth export controls have created urgent strategic demand for ex-China critical minerals capacity. Copper’s long-term deficit story remains intact regardless of the Q1 pullback.

In gold, the March correction notwithstanding, the structural pillars — central bank buying, ETF inflows, and a mining sector now generating record free cash flow — remain firmly in place.

Geopolitical risk insurance is a core tenet of this Fund. The combination of structurally constrained supply, rising strategic demand and still-attractive valuations — energy and materials remain among the cheapest sectors in global equity markets on earnings and cash flow metrics — continues to underpin our constructive long-term view. Natural resource equities do what they are designed to do when they are supposed to do it. Q1 2026 was exactly such a moment.
