data.title: Taiwan Semiconductor: Massive Growth From This Huge AI Winner
data.description:
Article Thesis

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM) reported very strong earnings results on Thursday. The company continues to execute very well, benefits from AI tailwinds, has a lot of pricing power, and remains far from expensive—all in all, I continue to like TSM a lot as a long-term investment.

Past Coverage

I have written about Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company here on Seeking Alpha multiple times in the past, most recently in January, when I published this article. I was bullish on TSM back then, primarily due to its strong growth, which has worked out well so far, as the three-month return of 7% is several times as high as the return of the broad market over the same time frame. With TSM reporting its Q1 earnings results on Thursday, it's time for me to update my views on this chip giant.

What Happened?

Early on Thursday, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing released the following earnings results:

We see that the company beat the consensus estimate on both lines, which naturally was great news. I wasn't too surprised, however, as the company had delivered a double beat during the previous 11 quarters as well—Wall Street analysts have a clear tendency to underestimate TSM, so odds of another double beat were high going into the report. The company beat the revenue consensus by a little more than 1%, which is solid, while the earnings per share beat was more sizeable, at around 3%. The market nevertheless reacted negatively to the report, with shares pulling back a little—but I think this may be a case of “selling the news”: TSM's shares were up more than 20% so far this year going into the earnings release, so it's not surprising that some investors decided to take some profits.

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company's Q1: Excellent Performance

Let's delve into TSM's results. The company's revenues were up by 41%, which is an excellent growth rate for any company—but for a company as large as Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company, this growth rate is outstanding. After all, growing revenues by more than 40% meant adding more than $10 billion in quarterly revenue in just one year, which is, I believe, quite a feat.

Growth was, of course, driven by headwinds from the ongoing artificial intelligence boom. Companies such as NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA), Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD), and Broadcom Inc. (AVGO) sell more and more AI chips to all kinds of customers, but primarily the hyperscalers like Amazon.com (AMZN) and Meta Platforms, Inc. (META). Since NVIDIA, AMD, Broadcom, and many other chip companies don't manufacture chips themselves but rely on foundries, these foundries can generate very attractive growth in the current environment—and with TSM being the biggest and most important foundry by far, it benefits a lot. It is hard to forecast how long the current AI boom will continue and what growth rates will look like next year, as there is some worry about ever-growing AI capex that may not result in attractive ROIs for the companies that use these AI chips. But at least for now, the AI boom is in full force, and TSM is extremely well-positioned to benefit from that, as it has a very strong market position in advanced processes such as 3 nm and 5 nm. To me, it's thus not surprising that these high-end processes are where TSM is generating a lot of its growth from, as we can see in the following chart:

Looking at 3 nm, 5 nm, and 7 nm revenue, we see that all three are growing, but the growth rate is better the “younger” or smaller the process is—5 nm is growing faster than 7 nm, and 3 nm is showing the best growth rate among the three on a year-over-year basis.

When we look at the sales mix on an end product basis, we see that HPC, high-performance computing, continues to grow quickly, with quarter-over-quarter growth at a very strong 20%—which isn't a surprise, as this is where the AI tailwinds are the largest. Since this is also TSM's biggest unit, with around 60% of overall revenue being generated here, the high relative growth rate of this unit means that TSM's company-wide growth is getting a major boost. Other areas are showing some growth as well, but are significantly less important for the company's overall growth rate for now—TSM's growth is an AI and data center story to a large degree, while other areas, such as automotive or smartphones, are reliable cash cows but aren't as exciting.

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing's market position as the biggest foundry with the best processes means that its customers are very reliant on TSM, especially for high-end chips. This gives TSM a lot of pricing power, of course, which is good for its revenues but even better for its margins—by raising prices over time, TSM can capture better and better gross margins. During the first quarter, TSM turned its 35% revenue growth rate (in Taiwanese dollars, 41% in USD) into a 52% gross profit increase thanks to gross margin growth of more than 700 base points—an excellent margin growth rate. Its customers, such as NVDA, still earn huge amounts of money and thus seemingly don't have any problem with TSM increasing its prices over time, so I wouldn't be surprised if this trend continues.

With TSM growing rapidly, it can generate some scale and operating leverage advantages, which is why its operating profit grew even faster than its gross profit: Operating profits were up by 62% during the first quarter, relative to one year earlier, in Taiwanese dollars. TSM's tax rate was up slightly versus one year earlier, but net profits and earnings per share were still up by around 60% in Taiwanese dollars and by 65% in US dollars—a growth rate that trounces most of the Magnificent 7 companies.

TSM keeps investing heavily in its operations to increase production capacity, which is why its free cash flows grew less than its profits, but free cash flows were still up by around 20% from one year earlier, which is pretty solid. And since the major capital expenditures today should allow for a lot of business growth in the coming years, I'm happy with free cash flow growing at a lower rate compared to profits for now.

Is Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company A Good Investment?

TSM's Q1 report was, I believe, really strong: analyst estimates were once again beaten on both lines, growth remains strong, especially in the high-margin AI and data center space, pricing power is excellent and allows for growing margins, and despite hefty investments, even TSM's free cash flow is growing.

And yet, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company trades at only 24x net earnings, which seems far from high. Considering the Q1 beat and TSM's history of outperforming expectations, current analyst estimates for this year are likely too low, I believe, which is why the “true” earnings multiple is likely even lower than 24. For a company with an excellent market position, strong growth, a healthy balance sheet, and so on, this seems like a rather discounted valuation to me, which is why I see further upside potential for TSM's shares both in 2026 and beyond.

That being said, we shouldn't completely ignore the risks: TSM is exposed to geopolitics and any potential Taiwan conflict escalation, and due to its exposure to consumer markets such as smartphones and automobiles, it could be hurt if the world were to slide into a recession. It makes sense to keep an eye on these potential risks, I believe, but I don't see them as so big that they make me avoid TSM—instead, I remain long.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Philip Morris: Expect Upside Surprise From Q1 2026 Results
data.description:
Here's something to note about the tobacco giant Philip Morris International Inc. (PM). The absolute daily average change in its stock price over the past year is 1.17%. By comparison, the average change on the day of its results release has been 3.8%. In three of the four result events in the past year, the change was greater than the annual average.

Next week, the company releases its Q1 2026 earnings. The trends over the last year indicate a high probability of a trading opportunity. My estimates for the forthcoming numbers further confirm the likelihood, as they can deliver an upside surprise. This is only supported by the stock's medium-to-long-term potential.

Estimating key Q1 2026 figures

Philip Morris has an edge over the rest of the tobacco companies in that it has definitively left all others behind, with the smoke-free business [SFB] bringing in over 40% of its revenues. If there's a jump in this contribution from SFB in Q1 2026, that alone is a reason for a rise.

Company guidance for 2026 forms the base

But there's more, as my estimates indicate, which, in turn, can reflect an above-average price increase this time around too. To lay the groundwork for the estimates for Q1 2026, here's a quick look at the company's key outlook figures for 2026:

  • Organic revenue growth is expected to be at 6% at the midpoint of the guidance range, a slight slowing down from the 6.5% increase seen last year.
  • At the midpoint, organic operating profit growth is seen at 8%, down from 10.6% in 2025, and growth in adjusted diluted EPS is expected to cool off to 12.1% at the midpoint, from 14.8% last year.

Estimates for Q1 2026

To get to the figures for Q1 2026, I've assumed, first, that both the revenue and the adjusted EPS will grow at the midpoint of the company's guidance range. And second, the quarterly figures will be the average of the percentage contribution seen in the past three years during the quarter. This yields the following results:

  • Revenue: The company is projected to report 23% of its revenues in the first quarter. With full-year revenue growth assumed to be at 6%, in Q1 2026, the figure would land at USD 9.9 billion. This represents a 6.4% YoY increase.
  • Adjusted EPS: With the average contribution to EPS in Q1 2026 at 26.6% over the last three years, the absolute number would land at USD 2.17 in the quarter. This would be an impressive 23.5% YoY increase.

Why Q1 2026 can surprise on the upside

My estimates are more bullish than the consensus of analysts' estimates on Seeking Alpha. The revenue growth estimate, for example, is half a percentage point higher than the 5.9% YoY increase seen by analysts.

The adjusted EPS growth is even more optimistic, with analysts expecting just an 8.4% YoY increase. As a side note, with my estimates showing over 20% YoY increase, the wide gap in adjusted EPS estimates bears more explaining. First, note that my full-year projection is actually 2.6% below analysts' estimates. The bigger estimate for Q1 2026 is balanced by a far more muted forecast for Q4 2026, which is smaller by as much as 46% compared to analysts' estimates.

Coming back to the main point. While the revenue surprises over the past four quarters have been both positive and negative, the EPS is a different story. Here's how:

  • Just over the past four quarters, the EPS has delivered an upside surprise, on average, by 3.6%.
  • Moreover, it has either surprised upwards or been on point for the past two years (see chart below).
  • And finally, only once in the past 12 quarters has the figure been less than expected.

Admittedly, even then, my estimate, which is 18.6% higher than the consensus figure, looks high. The last time almost as big a gap was seen between actuals and estimates was in Q2 2022, and it happened only once in the past four years. But it has happened. Moreover, the fact remains that the potential for upside is there, whether it's to the extent indicated by my estimates.

Stock metrics support a longer-term uptick

The medium-to-long-term forward P/E also supports the potential for an uptick, even as the short-term reflects that, broadly, the stock is more likely to stay unmoved in 2026. Here's how.

The adjusted EPS based on the company's guidance for 2026 results in a forward non-GAAP P/E of 19.1x. This is lower than the 20.2x levels it was at when I last checked. However, it remains slightly higher than the stock's five-year average of 17.8x.

Over the medium-to-long term, however, the stock's prospects look good. By 2028, for example, its forward P/E falls to 15.8x, and by 2030 the figure is at 13x. Since these are below the five-year average, they indicate price upside for the medium to long term, even if the stock is likely to stay flat this year.

The upside is also supported by the company's dividends, with its yield of 3.7%. The yield might not be the lowest among the big five tobacco stocks by market capitalisation, but there's something to be said for the fact that PM has grown dividends for the past 17 years.

What's next?

PM has had a lacklustre 2026 at the stock markets so far, with a 2.6% drop in price despite the market uncertainty that made both defensive and dividend stocks attractive for a time. The upcoming Q1 2026 results could provide a short-term break from the price inertia, as signs are strong for an uptick.

In the past year, the stock has seen a sharper single-day change than average on earnings releases. My estimates indicate the potential for a big upside surprise, which isn't even outside the realm of possibility, as past surprises indicate. Put together, and we have the potential for a price rise.

But going by the stock's fair valuation, it's unlikely to continue rising this year. But over the medium-to-long term, PM's market multiples and dividends both make a case for it. I'm retaining a Buy rating.

data.title: ARM: Why Investors Are Pricing In 'Immortality' As Technological Progress Hits A Ceiling
data.description:
Over the last year, the shares of ARM Holdings (ARM) flew up by more than 50%, taking the market capitalization of the company beyond the $170 billion mark. Driven by AI euphoria, the market has probably elevated this asset to the status of almost an indisputable idol. It appears that Wall Street relates to ARM not simply as a technological company, but seemingly as an eternal engine of growth.

Are we possibly looking at one of the most elegant business models in the history of capitalism? At the same time, the current price tag, at a forward P/E multiplier around 90x, implies that the company perhaps defeated not only competitors but also the very laws of physics. Investors are pricing in a utopian future, extrapolating current successes into infinity.

In this article, we will sort out the anatomy of this business and will show that the market most likely became a victim of an optical illusion. Investors pay a huge premium for a chart and loud headlines, ignoring the fact that the growth of the company is rigidly squeezed into the physical frames of the silicon epoch, which inexorably approaches its mathematical limit.

The Profile: Ideal Business and the "Tax on Silicon"

To understand why the market is so blindly in love with ARM, it is necessary to look at the company's product. ARM's main paradox is that it does not produce microcircuits. The company does not own multi-billion-dollar fabs, does not purchase silicon wafers, and does not engage in the manufacturing of transistors. Its only real asset is probably intellectual property.

If we imagine the process of the creation of a chip as a military operation, where transistors are soldiers, then ARM does not engage in the recruiting or training of infantry. ARM writes the Charter—the general plan of battle by which these soldiers are obliged to act.

The fundamental product of the company is the Instruction Set Architecture, or the base architecture of the instruction set. This is a peculiar "grammar", the foundation of the assembly language, on which software communicates with physical hardware. If a corporation of the level of Apple (AAPL) or Qualcomm (QCOM) wants to develop its own revolutionary chip, it cannot simply fabricate transistors however it sees fit. For millions of existing programs, applications, and operational systems to correctly work on this chip, the architecture must understand the standard instructions of ARM. For this right, companies pay huge licensing fees.

For smaller players, for example, manufacturers of IoT devices or household electronics, ARM plays the role of an architectural bureau, selling already-ready blueprints of processor cores from the Cortex line. You buy a ready-made plan of a "room", integrate it into your project, and send it to the factory.

Such a model creates a phenomenal economic moat. ARM is seemingly the key king of the silicon world. This is an intellectual toll road. Whoever produces a device is forced to pay a duty—a fixed percentage, or royalty, from every chip sold. It is unsurprising that the market perceives this business as a super-bond with a fantastic gross margin of 97%. This is a reliable, protected "tax on the IT-industry", which brings a free cash flow without the necessity to invest in heavy production.

The Problem of Scale: Mechanical Indicator of the Industry

If the business model looks flawless, what is the problem? The answer hides in the rigid boundaries of scaling.

For all its genius, the financial model of ARM works like a mechanical meter. The company's revenue is a derivative of the physical quantity of microcircuits produced in the world. Yes, it goes for aggressive expansion: besides their 99% share of the smartphone market, the architecture of ARM actively captures the automobile sector and server processors in data centers. But this process perhaps has its physical ceiling.

Given that ARM takes only a small royalty share from the cost of the final product, its potential for growth is tied to the fact that the world will physically produce more and more chips. This is the classic effect of a high base. The company already controls the dominant share of the market; therefore, it physically is probably not capable of growing by outstripping paces in relation to the entire semiconductor branch. To grow from $1 billion of revenue to $4 billion and capture the market of mobile devices was relatively easy. But to justify the current capitalization of $170 billion, this growth must continue by an exponent, which looks practically impossible in the real world.

The industry cannot, in a year, build five times more factories of TSMC or increase the world generation of electrical energy three times to feed an endless quantity of AI servers. The paces of growth of the IT sector, and that means also of the quantity of outputted chips, are stable but finite.

In essence, ARM is a barometer, measuring the temperature of the entire IT branch. If the shares of the company traded with a P/E in the area of 45x, this would be an absolutely fair and honest valuation of a high-quality asset, growing synchronously with the global market of technologies. No questions to the business would most likely have arisen.

At the same time, a multiplier of 90x changes the rules of the game. A forward P/E of 90x means that investors are pricing in astronomical, unrealistic volumes of future physical production or believe that clients like Apple, Google (GOOG), and NVIDIA (NVDA) will allow the company to endlessly raise the rates of royalty without resistance. In reality, though, an attempt to squeeze out too much from one's clients is a direct path to forcing them to push the transition to open, free standards, such as RISC-V. The market requires miracles from a mechanical meter, to which it is most likely not programmed.

Financial Reality: Signal of Braking

To dispel the magic of the market narrative, it is enough to look past the stock chart and look at the dry numbers from the last quarterly reports. If capitalization beats records, it is logical to expect that the financial flows of the company generate a waterfall of cash. But in reality the business of ARM has seemingly hit the brakes.

Revenue dynamics demonstrate a classic plateau. In March of 2025, ARM's quarterly revenue was $1.241 billion. After almost a year, in December 2025, this figure was at $1.242 billion. Three quarters in a row we observe an absolute "flatline" — movement by a straight line. The explosive growth of revenue, which the market laid down into the price of the share, de facto stopped.

An even more sobering signal arrives from net profit. For a year—from the quarter ending December 2024 to December 2025—net profit shrank from $252 million to $223 million. This is a fall of 11.5% year over year. What is the bottom line? For the last 12 months, the company generated about $4.67 billion of revenue and, in total, around $800 million of net profit. For this, the market generously values ARM at more than $170 billion. A dangerous divergence arises: investors pay 50% more, having grown the shares by 51.5% for a year, for an asset that began to earn less.

These numbers testify to our thesis: the effect of the "low base" is probably fully exhausted. The free fall of the paces of growth is confirmed by the financial reporting, and the business stabilized itself, having found its bottom of growth, in a phase of stagnation at high levels.

The Silicon Wall: The End of the Illusion of Immortality

One can justify such a monstrous breakaway of price from real cash flows only by one method—if one assumes that the architecture of ARM will exclusively dominate the market endlessly long. The purchase of shares with a multiplier P/E of 90x is perhaps the pricing of immortality. Investors lay down into the model that the company will collect its tax from the IT branch over the extent of almost a whole century. But here fundamental physics enters the game.

The entire architecture and ecosystem of ARM, its instruction set and assembler logic, is strictly optimized for a concrete physical environment—the management of electrons in silicon crystals. Over the extent of decades, the industry saved itself by Moore's Law, extensively decreasing the size of transistors and building up computing power. However, today we crash into the "silicon wall" at full speed.

Advanced tech processes already operate on scales of 1.5–2 nanometers. For the understanding of the physics of the process, the width of a single silicon atom constitutes about 0.2 nanometers. The channel of a transistor physically cannot become thinner than an atom. At the level of 0.5 nanometers, uncontrollable quantum effects of tunneling begin, and current begins spontaneously to "pierce" the insulation. The technology has practically come to a dead end; in other words, there is nowhere else to go anymore.

Attempts of the semiconductor industry to go into the third dimension, such as 3D chips and multi-story arrangements of transistors, are only to economize area on the PCB (printed circuit board). 3D stacking does not give a geometric progression of the growth of computing indicators; it only delays the inevitable. The physical ceiling of the current paradigm is already tangible.

It is worth it for investors to ask themselves a fundamental question: do you believe that the development of world electronics has reached its final peak? If you suppose that humanity forever will stop at the limit of one and a half nanometers and never again will accomplish a qualitative technological leap, then ARM is your ideal bet for the next 100 years. In a situation in which the development of electronics freezes and silicon remains with us forever, ARM will endlessly use its exclusive position. However, if you believe in progress, then the purchase of ARM at current prices possibly turns into a bet on an obviously losing strategy of stagnation.

The global demand for computing capacities will demand a multiple, thousand-fold increase of productivity, which inevitably will force the world to step over this limit and change the very physics of the process.

The Great Reset: A new paradigm

ARM's problem is that its architecture is not a universal law of nature but a code of rules, rigidly dictated by the physics of silicon. A transition to a new epoch of computations on the basis of the old "Charter" is most likely impossible. This reminds me of the situation with the internal combustion engine: you could possess all the patents in the world on how liquid fuel ignites in a cylinder, but in the moment of the arrival of the electric engine, these patents are zeroed out. The electric motor does not need your plan for the effective burning of gasoline. Exactly so, a photonic or quantum chip will not need an architecture created for the management of silicon transistors.

To provide the growth of productivity required for AI, it will be necessary for the industry to inevitably transition onto principally new physical principles. Be it photonic processors, carbon nanotubes, or neuromorphic systems, a transition to any of these technologies will provoke "The Great Reset", a great architectural reset.

What will occur with ARM at this moment? Their main asset, the instruction set ISA, will probably become a cumbersome and useless archaism. Investors often believe that software will not allow the market to quickly abandon the current architecture. However, an attempt to adapt old code and old logic to a wholly new physical environment always leads to a catastrophic fall in efficiency. We already saw this scenario unfold: when Intel (INTC) attempted to shove its dominant but heavy architecture, x86, into the first mobile devices, the adaptation failed, the efficiency turned out to be zero, and competitors momentarily filled the formed vacuum.

At the change of a fundamental technological paradigm, the old king loses all his privileges. A new, "native" architecture will be written for new physical chips. And in this new reality, ARM will not have a head start. Startups and cloud giants will begin a start from scratch, and the intellectual property, for which the market today pays $170 billion, risks turning into a legacy of the past.

Valuation & Conclusion

Summing up the financial outcome, it is critically important to separate the quality of the business itself from the optical illusion of its market valuation. ARM is really an outstanding company, fulfilling the role of the circulatory system of modern mobile and embedded electronics.

If the shares traded with a forward multiplier P/E in a conservative range of 40–45x, this would be an absolutely fair, honest valuation of a growing business. Such a multiplier would reflect the real state of affairs: the company develops synchronously with the global IT market.

But the current forward P/E on the level of 90x and a trailing P/E above 200x is perhaps the pricing of immortality. Buying the shares of ARM at these prices, you deprive your portfolio of the slightest margin of safety. You pay a premium for a scenario in which the laws of physics cease to act, and the competition from the side of the free standard RISC-V miraculously evaporates.

In conditions when quarterly revenue demonstrates stagnation and net profit declines year-over-year, a multiplier of 90x looks not like an indicator of growth but most likely like a bubble of expectations. While the market can believe in the endless future of the company for a very long time, I suppose that the shares are probably overvalued. I therefore assign a Hold rating on ARM stock.

For investors who already hold a position and benefited from the recent rally of 51%, now is an excellent moment to lock in profits. For new money, the current point of entry is mathematically not justified, in my view. I believe wise investors should probably wait for the normalization of multipliers and the cooling of the hype before making a bet on a company whose growth is rigidly limited by the physical quantity of silicon in the world.

data.title: It's A Bear Market Rally, The Selloff To Resume
data.description:
The bear market bounce

Technically, we are not in a bear market, but the current bounce looks like a bear market rally.

In fact, the major indices have been in a correction territory, 9% for S&P500 (SP500) and 14% for Nasdaq 100 (QQQ), going towards the technical 20% bear market threshold, but that selloff has been temporarily interrupted with a face-ripping rally to new all-time highs.

Furthermore, these "face-ripping" rallies usually occur during the bear markets and are usually led by the heavily shorted, fundamentally challenged stocks. That's exactly what happened during the April rally - it was led by software stocks (IGV), which face difficult fundamentals given the AI disruption.

Fundamentally, valuations are on par with the dot-com bubble. We are facing a severely adverse scenario - inflationary shock, which is likely to produce demand destruction and a recession. With respect to systemic credit risk, we are facing a private credit crisis, with significant bank exposure.

On top of everything, GDP is already slowing down to a crawl, only 0.5% in Q4 2025 and estimated at 1.3% for Q1 2026, driven mostly by AI investment - while the AI capex has an unknown ROI, which increases the bust risks. At the same time, inflation has been sticky and elevated even before the energy price shock.

Put it all together: fundamentals are deteriorating, valuations are extreme, there is an early-stage credit event, and there is an unfolding major inflationary geopolitical shock - that's a bear market environment.

So, this is nothing new; everybody is aware of these fundamentals - and that's a problem. Logically, given the current environment, an active trader would short the market. This is even more obvious if there is an unfolding largest energy price shock ever, with no end in sight.

Well, this only "end in sight" is the political narrative that "the end is in sight".

So, now combine the massive short positions (in reaction to the fundamentals) with the narrative that "the war in Iran will end soon" and "oil prices will fall sharply" - and you get a massive short-covering rally, driven by the heavily shorted stocks.

That's what happened, in my opinion, and I have been warning that it is dangerous to short stocks in the current environment.

Here is the chart of the S&P 500 with a face-ripping vertical rally in April.

The Iran war

Obviously, the key event moving the market now is the Iran war. The Strait of Hormuz is closed, and oil has been trading near $100/barrel. If this continues for a month or two, we are likely to get closer to the severely adverse scenario, defined by the Fed and the IMF as the inflationary led recession.

So, the rally has been triggered by the expectations of the ceasefire, and accelerated with the actual 2-week ceasefire and further accelerated with the expectations of a possible deal before the ceasefire expires.

The logic is that the deal will cause the price of oil to fall, and thus the inflationary shock will diminish, which will allow the Fed to continue cutting.

This is only a hope, as it will be difficult to make a compromise where Iran gets to retain the threshold state status and some nuclear leverage, while Israel demands no nuclear activity at all. The US would have to make significant long-term security guarantees with significant financial rewards for Iran, which is also unlikely.

Thus, there will likely be a ceasefire extension, but the Strait of Hormuz is likely to be at least partially obstructed indefinitely, which points to a more persistent inflation, and thus a constrained Fed.

The Software stocks

The rally has been led by software stocks up by 14%, where Oracle (ORCL) bounced by almost 30%, and Microsoft (MSFT) bounced by almost 15%. Note, Microsoft accounts for almost 5% of the S&P 500 Index (SPY).

Software stocks are disrupted by AI adoption - nothing fundamentally changed in April, and nothing positive will change anytime soon, AI tools are disrupting the fundamentals of the software industry, so this is not a fundamental rally.

The private credit crisis is directly related to the software industry, and thus alternative managers are also vulnerable. During the current bounce, Blue Owl (OWL) spiked by 20% - again nothing fundamental about the rally either.

The Mag 7 stocks

The S&P 500 can't rally without the Mag 7 stocks, given their weight in the Index. Overall, Mag 7 ETF (MAGS) rallied by almost 18%.

However, the Mag 7 stocks are out of favor now. The AI capex is massive, but without the guaranteed ROI, there are massive obstacles like energy costs, and most importantly, their collective cash flows are declining and debt is increasing. While, their collective accounting profits are still strong, under the surface the problems are emerging.

The valuation

The Shiller PE ratio for the S&P 500 is at 40.67, just below the record valuation during the dot-com era. So, based on this measure, it's a bubble.

Implications

Based on the Shiller P/E ratio, the S&P 500 is a bubble. But, bubbles need a trigger to burst. The currently unfolding inflationary shock could be the trigger that bursts the bubble. Rising inflation constrains the Fed, and tightens credit together with the private credit crisis, which causes demand destructions - and the end result is a bear market with a bubble bust and a systemic credit event. That's basically a bear market on par with 2008, over a 50% drawdown.

The counterargument is that President Trump will not allow this to happen - any time that market starts pricing an adverse scenario, Trump jawbones the market with a positive narrative, and even backs down from some policies. The result is a market bounce due to short covering, and speculative buying.

This positive narrative can continue to affect the market as long as the data stays positive. Once inflation data starts showing persistent pressures and companies start warning about margin pressures, the narrative will become less effective - that's when the market selloff accelerates.

Most imminently, we are facing a ceasefire deadline next week, and in all likelihood the ceasefire will be extended, but this will not alleviate inflationary pressures as long as there is no comprehensive peace deal.

However, even with the deal, investors are facing issues with the tech sector (due to AI disruption and AI capex) and the financial sector (due to private credit).

In my opinion, the peace deal is unlikely, and oil is likely to remain near $100 for the foreseeable future, and we are likely to revisit the lows near 6300 for the S&P 500 as inflation continues to rise during the summer, before the selloff continues to the 20% bear market threshold and exceeds it.

data.title: Quality As A Foundation In An Uncertain World
data.description:
By Ayush Babel and Hyun Kang

Investors are navigating an increasingly complex market backdrop. Geopolitical tensions, ongoing conflicts and energy supply risks continue to add volatility, while rapid technological change, particularly the acceleration of artificial intelligence, is reshaping industries, business models, and sources of competitive advantage.

Against this backdrop of geopolitical uncertainty and technological disruption, identifying companies with durable business models and resilient fundamentals has become increasingly important.

Historically, companies with strong profitability, balance sheet strength, and disciplined capital allocation—characteristics commonly associated with the quality factor—have demonstrated some of the most consistent performance across market cycles. Compared with other equity factors, quality has tended to outperform more frequently over long investment horizons while experiencing relatively shallow periods of underperformance.

Quality Has Shown the Greatest Resilience, Outperforming More Often Than Other Factors

Figure 1: Rolling 10-Year outperformance of U.S. Factors (vs. Market)

Figure 2: Factor Performance Summary

Sources: WisdomTree, Ken French, data as of November 28, 2025. Value: High 30% Book to Price portfolio. Size: Low 30% portfolio. Quality: High 30% portfolio. Low Vol: Low 20% portfolio. High Div: High 30% portfolio. "&" Market: all CRSP firms incorporated in the U.S. and listed on the NYSE, AMEX or NASDAQ. Past performance is not indicative of future results.

The importance of quality becomes even clearer when examining how profitability interacts with valuation and growth characteristics.

Outperformance of Quality Across the Value-Growth Spectrum

Figure 3: Returns by Profitability Across Value and Growth Segments

Source: Kenneth French Data Library, 6/30/63-2/28/2026. Period based on availability of annual operating profitability returns sorted into quintiles, which begins 6/30/1963. Market is U.S.-listed equities grouped on the basis of operating profitability and price-to-book. Returns are annualized. Past performance is not indicative of future results.

Companies with higher operating profitability have historically outperformed lower-quality peers across the entire growth-value spectrum, with the strongest effect observed among blend stocks. In other words, sustainable growth is often supported by strong fundamentals.

The WisdomTree Quality Dividend Growth Approach

The WisdomTree U.S. Quality Dividend Growth Fund (DGRW) seeks to capture this quality premium through a forward-looking approach to dividend investing.

Rather than selecting companies purely on the basis of high dividend yields, the strategy emphasizes firms with the potential to grow their dividends over time. The process focuses on companies with strong profitability metrics and earnings growth characteristics that can support sustainable dividend growth.

The strategy also differs from traditional market capitalization-weighted approaches by weighting companies according to the dividends they pay. This dividend-weighted methodology helps reduce concentration in the largest companies and links portfolio weights more closely to the cash returned to shareholders.

By combining quality screens with a forward-looking dividend growth framework, the strategy aims to identify companies capable of delivering both growing income and long-term capital appreciation.

Conclusion: Exposure Rooted in Fundamentals

Ultimately, the case for quality is not just theoretical—it is clearly reflected in portfolio fundamentals. As shown in the chart below, the WisdomTree U.S. Quality Dividend Growth Index exhibits meaningfully higher profitability metrics than the S&P 500 Index, with return on equity and return on assets notably elevated. At the same time, this higher quality exposure is not achieved at the expense of excessive valuation, with forward price-to-earnings (P/E) broadly in line with the market and only a modest premium on price-to-book (P/B).

Figure 4: Fundamental Comparison

Sources: WisdomTree, FactSet. Data as of 3/31/2026.

Importantly, the strategy also delivers a higher forward dividend yield, reinforcing the idea that investors do not need to sacrifice income to gain exposure to stronger businesses. Instead, they can access a combination of profitability, sustainable growth and income generation within a single framework.

In an uncertain and rapidly evolving market environment, this balance becomes increasingly valuable. WisdomTree’s U.S. Quality Dividend Growth strategy provides an approach rooted in fundamentals, one that seeks to capture resilient earnings, disciplined capital allocation, and growing shareholder returns. For investors looking to navigate volatility while maintaining long-term return potential, anchoring portfolios in high-quality businesses may offer a more consistent and dependable path forward.

Important Risks Related to this Article

There are risks associated with investing, including possible loss of principal. Growth stocks, as a group, may be out of favor with the market and underperform value stocks or the overall equity market. Growth stocks are generally more sensitive to market movements than other types of stocks. Dividends are not guaranteed, and a company currently paying dividends may cease paying dividends at any time. The Fund is non-diversified, as a result, changes in the market value of a single security could cause greater fluctuations in the value of Fund shares than would occur in a diversified fund. The Fund invests in the securities included in, or representative of, its Index regardless of their investment merit and the Fund does not attempt to outperform its Index. The composition of the Index is governed by an Index Committee and the Index may not perform as intended. Please read the Fund’s prospectus for specific details regarding the Fund’s risk profile.

Ayush Babel, Director, Quantitative Research

Ayush Babel is the Director of Quantitative Research in WisdomTree's multi-asset quantitative research and index teams. In this role, he focuses on developing innovative quantitative strategies across various asset classes while supporting WisdomTree's diverse range of products. His expertise spans factor exploration, portfolio construction and optimization, quantitative investment research, and product development.

With over a decade of experience in the financial services industry, Ayush has held investment research roles at J.P. Morgan and Franklin Templeton. At these institutions, he was responsible for developing and managing equity and fixed income smart beta products, as well as cross-asset risk premia solutions for global institutional and retail clients. His experience covers a broad spectrum of asset classes and investment styles.

Ayush holds a bachelor's in Engineering Physics and a master’s degree in Nanoscience from the Indian Institute of Technology, Bombay.

Hyun Kang, Research Analyst

Hyun Kang joined WisdomTree in July 2022 as a Research Analyst. As a part of the Index team, he assists with the creation and maintenance of the firm’s indexes and supports the group’s research initiatives across various strategies. Hyun graduated from Carnegie Mellon University, with a B.S. in Business Administration and an additional major in Statistics and Machine Learning.

data.title: J.B. Hunt Transport Services: Valuation Seems Rich At The Current Level
data.description:
Investment action

I had a hold rating for J.B. Hunt Transport Services, Inc. (JBHT) previously, as I expected the lack of volume recovery to continue weighing on valuation multiples despite the cost-reduction story playing out nicely. The fundamental setup has changed. Q1 2026 finally showed real revenue growth, better Intermodal trends, and early signs that the freight market is improving. More importantly, JBHT is entering this phase with a leaner cost base, which should help operating leverage if the recovery continues. However, valuation has gone up quite significantly too. At the current multiple, I don't see much of an upside unless JBHT shows really strong adj. EPS growth.

1Q26 earnings review

JBHT's Q1 2026 gave a pleasant surprise on revenue growth. Total operating revenue was ~$3.06 billion, up ~5.1% y/y. Even after stripping out fuel surcharge, operating revenue was still $2.64 billion, up 3% y/y. The segment picture was better too. Intermodal revenue was ~$1.51 billion, up ~2.4% y/y. Loads increased 2.9% y/y, while revenue per load moderated slightly by 0.4% y/y. Management also said intermodal volumes were up 3% y/y; Dedicated revenue was up 2.2% y/y to $841 million; ICS revenue growth was even more solid at 20.4% y/y, driving total revenue to $323 million; Truckload [JBT] revenue did well too at $205 million, up 23.3% y/y; and lastly, Final Mile revenue was the only weak segment that saw negative growth of 6.3% y/y, down to $188 million. As for profitability, EBIT grew 15.9% y/y to $207 million, and the operating ratio [OR] improved to 93.2% from 93.9% a year ago. By segments, Intermodal EBIT grew to $114 million; Dedicated stayed steady at $87 million; while the weaker spots were Integrated Capacity Solutions [ICS], where it saw -$5 million EBIT. Overall, net income still grew to $142 million, and diluted EPS grew to $1.49 from $1.17.

Revenue growth is finally here

Previously, my view was that JBHT was executing well, but the broader freight market was still too weak to support real top-line growth. At that time, I believed my argument made sense since revenue was flat and volumes were mixed. This is no longer the case. Revenue grew. Intermodal volumes grew. JBT revenue grew sharply. ICS revenue grew sharply too. In my view, these are the first signs of a market that is getting better. Of all this, perhaps Intermodal is the best evidence we need. Recall back in Q3 2025, Transcon was down 6%, and total JBI volumes were down 1%. This time, management talked about record first-quarter volume, a record week in March, broad-based strength across the network, and Eastern load growth of ~7%. Even Transcon was no longer in decline (was roughly flat this quarter).

Management's tone also changed for the better. The key message I took away was that management said the freight environment felt meaningfully different from what they had seen over the last few years, with tighter capacity and early demand improvement. Of course, we cannot just trust management's words alone, but if we combine that with the latest quarter's better volume, better revenue, and better segment trends, I believe it has changed the debate. We are starting to see the first signs of recovery, and if this trend continues, the stock should start to price in a recovery ahead.

The fundamental setup leans bullish

Demand gradually recovering is definitely good for the equity story, but it gets better when we consider that JBHT kept getting leaner. Management said in the call that JBHT saved another $30 million of annual costs in Q1, taking annual cost savings to a little over $130 million (this is above the prior $100 million target). The context shifted for the better because those savings are no longer seen as actions to protect earnings in a soft market. Now they do more than that. If revenue is starting to move again, a lower cost base should mean better operating leverage from here, which means we could see a structurally lower OR and faster earnings growth.

We have other data points that support a bullish setup too. For instance, in Dedicated, while the quarter itself was solid, the more important point is what it says about demand. Management said interest in the service is picking up, and JBHT has sold 295 trucks in Q1 while staying on track for 800 to 1,000 new trucks for 2026. This is a very bullish data point because it says customers are feeling the tightness in capacity and are turning to JBHT for solutions. There is some lag here because these new deals have start-up costs hitting the P&L before we see impact at the EBIT line. But this is really not a bad problem to have, as it usually means demand is coming in.

Even the weaker segments also point to an early recovery cycle. It is true that ICS still lost money, and JBT's gross profit was pressured by higher fuel costs and tighter capacity, which pushed up purchased transportation costs. But the point is these do not suggest weak demand. The problem is that costs went up faster than what they could charge customers. Think of it like this: these businesses are paying more to move the freight, but they have not fully raised their prices yet. That can happen early in a recovery. In a weak market, ICS and JBT usually struggle because there is not enough freight to move. Here, the issue looks different. There seems to be freight demand, but profits are being squeezed because costs moved first and pricing is still catching up. What JBHT needs to do here is to start raising prices to reflect the current demand/supply situation, which I don't doubt is doable if we go into an upcycle.

Valuation

In the past 2 decades, JBHT has seen strong adj. EPS growth during the first few years of every cycle recovery (ranging from 20+% to even 50+%). Consensus is currently pricing 20%+ adj. EPS growth over the next two years, which I think could be too conservative if we see a strong recovery, given JBHT's operating cost is lower today.

I think the market has run ahead to start pricing in a recovery. We can see this in the forward PE multiple, now at ~30.5x, which is way ahead of its past 10-year trading history, and adj. EPS growth to be >20% for the next two years. Like I have shown previously, this level of adj. EPS growth is not surprising, and there could be upside considering the lower cost today. Consensus estimates have also been fairly accurate (<4% range on average) over the years. My view on valuing such cyclical companies is to adopt a through-cycle valuation methodology. The question to ask is, "What multiple would JBHT trade in a normalized environment?". Let's assume that FY2029 is where things start to normalize; at that point, JBHT should trade at its historical average (my benchmark for "through-cycle"), which is ~21.7x. Using a consensus estimate of ~$11.44 for FY2029 adj. EPS and multiplying that by 21.7x, I get to a share price target of $248, which is really not that far from where JBHT is today.

For investors to see any meaningful upside here, JBHT has to beat consensus estimates and show a much stronger adj. EPS growth. If not, I think valuation is going to stay capped at this multiple.

Conclusion

I give a hold rating to JBHT. The recovery cycle seems to be happening. At the same time, JBHT is still benefiting from structural cost savings, which should support stronger earnings growth if the cycle keeps improving. There are still areas that need better pricing, but I think the direction of travel has clearly turned more favorable. However, valuation has run ahead and is not cheap if we assume multiples to normalize back to historical average.
